NVDA.title: Samsung Posts Record Profit As AI Memory Boom Drives Chip Surge

macro_events.event_summary: Volkswagen Q1 profit fell 14% due to US tariff pressure and intensifying Chinese EV competition.
primary_drivers: US auto tariff cost absorption squeezing foreign OEM margins
Chinese domestic EV makers taking share from legacy Western brands in China
Read-across to other European/Japanese OEMs with US import exposure

beneficiaries.rationale: Predominantly US-domestic production footprint vs VW; gains relative competitive positioning as tariffs hurt European imports. Also benefits from VW ceding ground but GM's own China JV (SAIC-GM) is a partial offset.
Similar to GM — heavy US manufacturing base means less tariff exposure than VW; Ford's truck/SUV mix has minimal overlap with VW's tariff-hit imports, allowing relative share gain in US.
Direct beneficiary in two ways: (1) US-built vehicles avoid import tariffs that hit VW's ID4/Audi lineup, (2) competes directly with VW EVs in Europe and US where VW is now margin-constrained.

losers.rationale: Stellantis has very similar profile to VW: European-headquartered OEM with significant US import exposure (Jeep, Ram partially, plus Fiat/Alfa/Maserati fully imported) and weak China position. Tariff read-across is direct.
Toyota — large share of US sales are imports from Japan; same tariff transmission mechanism, though Toyota has more US plants than VW so impact is partial.
Honda — meaningful US import share (especially Acura, CR-V from Canada/Mexico/Japan); tariff regime is a shared headwind highlighted by VW's miss.
Aptiv supplies VW group heavily; lower VW production volumes and margin pressure flow through to component pricing/volumes for tier-1 suppliers.
Magna is a major contract manufacturer/supplier to European OEMs including VW Group; weaker VW volumes and pricing pressure compress supplier orders.

headlines: Volkswagen posts 14% drop in first-quarter profit on tariff pressure, China competition

macro_events.event_summary: Oil prices surge on Iran-related news pressuring European equities lower ahead of ECB and BOE rate decisions.
primary_drivers: Crude oil price spike lifts E&P revenue and refining crack spreads
Geopolitical risk premium boosts defense and safe-haven demand
Higher energy input costs squeeze margins for fuel-intensive consumers (airlines, chemicals)

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with ~70% upstream earnings leverage; every $10/bbl move in Brent adds materially to quarterly EPS. Direct beneficiary of oil price surge.
Highest-beta US large-cap E&P to crude prices given Permian pure-play exposure and elevated leverage; trades as a leveraged bet on WTI.
Significant Egyptian/North Sea production gives direct Brent exposure; small-cap E&P with high oil-price torque.
Iran-related geopolitical escalation typically drives defense rotation; Lockheed has direct missile defense (THAAD, PAC-3) exposure to Middle East tensions.
Patriot missile and Tomahawk maker with material Middle East FMS pipeline; benefits from sustained Iran risk premium.
Refiner with high complexity; supply-disruption-driven crude spikes often widen product cracks more than crude rises, expanding margins.

losers.rationale: Jet fuel is ~25-30% of operating costs and Delta has limited hedging; oil surge directly compresses margins. Pure-play exposure to higher crude.
Same fuel-cost transmission as DAL; United runs largely unhedged and has high international exposure where ticket price elasticity is greater.
Southwest historically hedged but current book is light; fuel is its largest variable cost and domestic competition limits fare passthrough.
Ethylene/petrochemical feedstock costs tied to naphtha and natural gas liquids; sustained oil surge compresses chemical margins.
Olefins producer with direct hydrocarbon feedstock exposure; margin squeeze when crude rises faster than downstream polymer prices.
Cruise operator with fuel as ~10-15% of costs and no pricing power mid-season; oil spike hits margins and consumer discretionary travel demand simultaneously.

headlines: European markets set for lower open as oil prices surge; ECB and BOE decisions ahead
